macro_segments.title: Designating a Slaughtered Chattat: Core Principle That a Korban Must Match the Specific Person and Sin

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Opening Case: A Son Cannot Use His Father's Chattat After the Father Dies
Reasoning: No Kapara for the Dead, and a Chattat Cannot Be Used for a Different Person or Sin
Biblical Source from Vayikra: Korbano Al Chataso—His Korban for His Sin, Not Another's
The Gemara's First Challenge: Can a Son Use His Father's Money If Designated Generically?
Distinction Between Animal and Money Offerings: Fixed Kedusha by Animals Versus Transferable Kedusha by Money
The Concept of Me'ila: Why a Fixed Designation Cannot Be Switched, Even for the Same Person's Different Sins

macro_segments.title: Flexibility Within One Korban Type: Switching Animals and Moving Up or Down the Ola VeYored Scale

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Secondary Cases: Switching from Sheep to Goat and Back, Generic to Generic
Ola VeYored Flexibility: Moving Down When Poorer (Bird or Minca) and Up When Wealthier
The Gemara's Derivation from Mechataso and Al Chataso: Subset and Addition Language in Vayikra
Why Both 'From Sheep' and 'From Bird' Must Be Stated: Avoiding Inference That Minca-to-Bird Is Blocked
Exception: Birds Cannot Move Down to Minca Because Birds Lack Pidyon (Redemption)

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Dichuy: When a Korban Is Set Aside at the Wrong Economic Level, It Remains Forever Rejected

micro_segments.title: Case: A Rich Person Sets Aside Birds When They Should Set Aside a Sheep—Dichuy Remains Permanent
The Gemara's Shema Mina Telat: Three Surprising Conclusions About Ba'alei Chayim and Kedusha Types
Rebuttal from Rabbi Shimon: A Misbirth (Female Pesach) Giving Birth to a Male Shows Ba'alei Chayim Are Not Nidchin
Counter-Rebuttal: Rav Shimi Bar Ashi on the Hagralah Case—When Dichuy Is and Isn't Permanent

macro_segments.title: The Timing of Bird Designation: When Kinnim Are Separated Into Distinct Chatas and Ola Identities

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Principle: Kinnim Separate Only at Purchase or at Kohen's Action, Never in Between
Proof from Vayikra: Two Phrases 'Echa Le-Chatas, Echa Le-Ola' Define Designation Moments
Challenge from the Hagralah Case: Lots Define Yom Kippur Goats Outside Normal Purchase-Kohen Times
Analysis of the Kal VaChomer: Why Even Verbal Designation Should Work for the Goats, Yet It Does Not
Resolution: The Verse Says Only the Lot Works, Not Verbal Designation, for Yom Kippur's Special Case

macro_segments.title: Subdividing Bird Money After Wealth Change: Can Money Designated Before Remain Valid if Wealth Status Shifts?

micro_segments.title: The Braita: Poor Person Sets Aside Bird Money, Then Becomes Rich—Can They Subdivide and Use Part?
Rav Sheshes' Objection: If Rich, Why Can They Designate Parts of Bird Money Post-Wealth Change?
Rav Elazar in the Name of Rabbi Oshaya: A Rich Person Cannot Fulfill with a Bird Even Post-Facto
Resolution: The Braita Means Designation Already Occurred at the Original Setting-Aside Time
Rav Chaga's Alternative: If Post-Facto Bird Works, Then Subdivision After Wealth Change Is Possible

macro_segments.title: Can One Move Down or Up the Ola VeYored Scale Post-Facto and by What Measure?

micro_segments.title: Braita on Metzora: Poor Person Bringing Richer Offering—Yotzei; Rich Bringing Poorer—Lo Yotzei
Grammatical Proof from Torah: 'Zos' (This Exact Way) vs. 'Toras' (General Teaching) on Metzora
Distinction from Normal Ola VeYored: By Metzora, Torah Explicitly Forbids Going Down; By Others, Debate Remains
Summary: Going Up Allowed Post-Facto; Going Down Forbidden by Metzora but Debated for Others

macro_segments.title: Comparative Rankings in Torah Language: Sheep vs. Goat, Dove vs. Pigeon, Father vs. Mother, Rav vs. Father

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Teaching: Torah Lists Sheep Before Goat Everywhere, But One Reversal Shows They Are Equal
Same Logic Applied: Dove and Pigeon, Father and Mother—Reversals Prove Equivalence, Not Hierarchy
Caveat on Father and Mother: Sages Override Equivalence When Wife Owes Kavod to Husband
Rav Over Father: Talmid Torah Deserves Greater Kavod When Studying from Rav Than Honoring Father

macro_segments.title: The Four Cryings of the Temple and the Fall of Issachar of Kfar Barkai: Cautionary Tales of Unworthy Service

micro_segments.title: First and Second Cryings: Expulsion of Eli's Sons and Welcome for Yochanan ben Nidbai's Gluttony
Third and Fourth Cryings: Welcome for Elishama ben Pikai and Expulsion of Issachar of Kfar Barkai
Issachar's Sin: Wearing Silk Gloves to Protect His Kavod Rather Than Serve the Korbanot Properly
The King's Judgment: Issachar's Dismissive Gesture Results in Both Hands Being Cut Off
Final Insult: Rav Ashi Notes Issachar Never Even Learned the Mishnah Teaching That Sheep and Goats Are Equal

macro_segments.title: Conclusion: The Power of Torah Learning and Peace Among Students of the Wise

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar in the Name of Rabbi Chanina: Students of the Wise Increase Peace in the World
